   273→  background: repeating-linear-gradient(
   274→    0deg,
   275→    rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15) 0px,
   276→    rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15) 1px,
   277→    transparent 1px,
   278→    transparent 3px
   279→  );
   280→}
   281→```
   282→
   283→Toggle via a small "CRT" button in the corner. Off by default.
   284→
   285→## Animations
   286→
   287→### Wagon Travel
   288→- Wagon centered horizontally
   289→- Landscape scrolls left behind the wagon
   290→- Wheel rotation synced to scroll speed
   291→- Gentle wagon bounce (1-2px vertical oscillation)
   292→
   293→### Arrival at Stop
   294→- Wagon decelerates (easing function)
   295→- Landmark appears and grows into view
   296→- Stop name banner slides down from top
   297→- Code panel slides up from bottom
   298→
   299→### Event Appearance
   300→- Screen flash (brief gold overlay)
   301→- Event panel slides in with border glow
   302→- Choices appear one by one with slight stagger (200ms each)
   303→
   304→### Answer Feedback
   305→- Correct: Green flash, choice pulses, health bar animates up, "+10" floats up
   306→- Wrong: Red flash, screen shake (2px, 200ms), health bar animates down, "-15" floats up
   307→- Party damage: Portrait flashes red, health pip disappears
   308→
   309→### Death Sequence
   310→- Screen slowly fades to dark
   311→- Tombstone rises from bottom center
   312→- Death message typewriter-animates onto tombstone
   313→- "Press any key" fades in after message completes
   314→
   315→### Win Sequence
   316→- Fireworks: random position colored pixel explosions (8-10 bursts)
   317→- Score card slides in from right
   318→- Stats count up from 0 to final values (satisfying animation)
   319→- Study recommendation typewriter-animates last
   320→
   321→## Sound (Web Audio API — Optional)
   322→
   323→All sounds are generated via Web Audio API oscillators. No audio files needed.
   324→
   325→| Event | Sound |
   326→|-------|-------|
   327→| Travel start | Rising 3-note arpeggio (C-E-G) |
   328→| Stop arrival | Descending chime (G-E-C) |
   329→| Correct answer | Happy jingle (C-E-G-C♯) |
   330→| Wrong answer | Descending buzz (low frequency) |
   331→| Party member death | Sad descending tone |
   332→| Win | Triumphant 8-bar melody |
   333→| Death | Low somber 4-note phrase |
   334→| River crossing | Water-like oscillation |
   335→| Event appear | Alert tone (2 quick beeps) |
   336→
   337→Toggle via a speaker icon in the corner. **Off by default** — opt-in.
   338→
   339→## Accessibility
   340→
   341→- All choice buttons are keyboard-navigable (Tab + Enter)
   342→- Arrow keys navigate choices
   343→- Code panel has proper contrast ratios (WCAG AA on dark backgrounds)
   344→- Status bar text has sufficient contrast
   345→- Screen reader: choices have aria-labels
   346→- No auto-playing sound
   347→

1→#!/bin/bash
     2→# scripts/tts.sh - Universal TTS wrapper for diff-pair-review
     3→# Supports macOS say (default) and OpenAI TTS API with automatic fallback
     4→set -euo pipefail
     5→
     6→SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
     7→PLUGIN_ROOT="$(dirname "$SCRIPT_DIR")"
     8→
     9→source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/lib/utils.sh"
    10→source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/lib/config.sh"
    11→
    12→# Defaults (overridden by config/env/flags)
    13→ENGINE="${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_ENGINE:-say}"
    14→VOICE="${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_VOICE:-}"
    15→RATE="${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_RATE:-200}"
    16→SPEED="${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_SPEED:-1.0}"
    17→MODEL="${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_MODEL:-tts-1}"
    18→DEBUG="${DIFF_REVIEW_DEBUG:-false}"
    19→TEMP_DIR="${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/diff-review-tts"
    20→TEXT=""
    21→
    22→# --- Engine implementations ---
    23→
    24→exec_say_tts() {
    25→  local text="$1"
    26→  local voice="$2"
    27→  local rate="$3"
    28→
    29→  if ! command -v say &>/dev/null; then
    30→    error "say command not found"
    31→    return 1
    32→  fi
    33→
    34→  debug "Executing: say -v '$voice' -r $rate"
    35→  say -v "$voice" -r "$rate" "$text" &
    36→}
    37→
    38→exec_openai_tts() {
    39→  local text="$1"
    40→  local voice="$2"
    41→  local model="$3"
    42→  local speed="$4"
    43→
    44→  if ! command -v curl &>/dev/null; then
    45→    error "curl not found"; return 1
    46→  fi
    47→  if ! command -v afplay &>/dev/null; then
    48→    error "afplay not found"; return 1
    49→  fi
    50→  if [[ -z "${OPENAI_API_KEY:-}" ]]; then
    51→    error "OPENAI_API_KEY not set"; return 1
    52→  fi
    53→
    54→  local temp_file="${TEMP_DIR}/tts_$(date +%s%N).mp3"
    55→  local escaped_text
    56→  escaped_text=$(printf '%s' "$text" | jq -Rs .)
    57→
    58→  debug "Calling OpenAI TTS API (model=$model, voice=$voice)"
    59→  local http_code
    60→  http_code=$(curl -s -w "%{http_code}" -o "$temp_file" \
    61→    https://api.openai.com/v1/audio/speech \
    62→    -H "Authorization: Bearer ${OPENAI_API_KEY}" \
    63→    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    64→    -d "{
    65→      \"model\": \"${model}\",
    66→      \"voice\": \"${voice}\",
    67→      \"input\": ${escaped_text},
    68→      \"speed\": ${speed}
    69→    }")
    70→
    71→  if [[ "$http_code" != "200" ]]; then
    72→    error "OpenAI API returned HTTP $http_code"
    73→    rm -f "$temp_file"
    74→    return 1
    75→  fi
    76→
    77→  if [[ ! -s "$temp_file" ]]; then
    78→    error "OpenAI API returned empty response"
    79→    return 1
    80→  fi
    81→
    82→  debug "Playing audio with afplay"
    83→  afplay "$temp_file" &
    84→  local afplay_pid=$!
    85→
    86→  # Clean up temp file after playback finishes
    87→  (wait "$afplay_pid" 2>/dev/null; rm -f "$temp_file") &
    88→}
    89→
    90→# --- Argument parsing ---
    91→
    92→while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    93→  case $1 in
    94→    --engine) ENGINE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    95→    --voice) VOICE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    96→    --rate) RATE="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    97→    --speed) SPEED="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    98→    --model) MODEL="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    99→    --debug) DEBUG=true; shift ;;
   100→    --help)
   101→      cat <<'EOF'
   102→Usage: tts.sh [OPTIONS] [TEXT]
   103→
   104→OPTIONS:
   105→  --engine ENGINE   say or openai (default: say)
   106→  --voice VOICE     Voice name (default: Samantha/alloy)
   107→  --rate RATE       Words per minute for say (default: 200)
   108→  --speed SPEED     Speed multiplier for openai 0.25-4.0 (default: 1.0)
   109→  --model MODEL     OpenAI model: tts-1 or tts-1-hd (default: tts-1)
   110→  --debug           Enable debug output
   111→
   112→ENVIRONMENT:
   113→  DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_ENGINE, DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_VOICE, DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_RATE
   114→  DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_SPEED, DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_MODEL, OPENAI_API_KEY
   115→EOF
   116→      exit 0
   117→      ;;
   118→    *) TEXT="$*"; break ;;
   119→  esac
   120→done
   121→
   122→# Read from stdin if no text argument
   123→if [[ -z "$TEXT" ]]; then
   124→  TEXT=$(cat)
   125→fi
   126→
   127→if [[ -z "$TEXT" ]]; then
   128→  error "No text provided"
   129→  exit 1
   130→fi
   131→
   132→# Load config file defaults (for anything not already set)
   133→load_config
   134→
   135→# Set default voice based on engine if still empty
   136→if [[ -z "$VOICE" ]]; then
   137→  if [[ "$ENGINE" == "openai" ]]; then
   138→    VOICE="alloy"
   139→  else
   140→    VOICE="Samantha"
   141→  fi
   142→fi
   143→
   144→# Create temp directory
   145→mkdir -p "$TEMP_DIR"
   146→
   147→# Cleanup on exit
   148→cleanup() { rm -rf "${TEMP_DIR}"/*.mp3 2>/dev/null || true; }
   149→trap cleanup EXIT
   150→
   151→# --- Execute ---
   152→
   153→case "$ENGINE" in
   154→  say)
   155→    exec_say_tts "$TEXT" "$VOICE" "$RATE"
   156→    ;;
   157→  openai)
   158→    if ! exec_openai_tts "$TEXT" "$VOICE" "$MODEL" "$SPEED"; then
   159→      warn "OpenAI TTS failed, falling back to macOS say"
   160→      exec_say_tts "$TEXT" "${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_VOICE:-Samantha}" "$RATE"
   161→    fi
   162→    ;;
   163→  *)
   164→    warn "Unknown engine '$ENGINE', falling back to say"
   165→    exec_say_tts "$TEXT" "Samantha" "$RATE"
   166→    ;;
   167→esac
   168→

1→#!/bin/bash
     2→# scripts/open-viz.sh - Write an HTML visualization file and open it in the browser
     3→# Used by /fly-visual to display the Coderegon Trail game
     4→set -euo pipefail
     5→
     6→SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
     7→PLUGIN_ROOT="$(dirname "$SCRIPT_DIR")"
     8→
     9→source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/lib/utils.sh"
    10→
    11→# Defaults
    12→OUTPUT_DIR="${PLUGIN_ROOT}/.diff-review"
    13→FILENAME=""
    14→OPEN_BROWSER=true
    15→
    16→# Parse arguments
    17→while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    18→  case $1 in
    19→    --output-dir) OUTPUT_DIR="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    20→    --filename) FILENAME="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    21→    --no-open) OPEN_BROWSER=false; shift ;;
    22→    --help)
    23→      cat <<'EOF'
    24→Usage: open-viz.sh [OPTIONS] < HTML_CONTENT
    25→
    26→Reads HTML content from stdin, writes it to a file, and opens it in the browser.
    27→
    28→OPTIONS:
    29→  --output-dir DIR   Directory to write the HTML file (default: .diff-review/)
    30→  --filename NAME    Output filename (default: trail-{timestamp}.html)
    31→  --no-open          Write file but don't open in browser
    32→
    33→EXAMPLE:
    34→  echo "<html>...</html>" | open-viz.sh --filename "trail-nextjs.html"
    35→  open-viz.sh --filename "trail-rails.html" < game.html
    36→EOF
    37→      exit 0
    38→      ;;
    39→    *) error "Unknown option: $1"; exit 1 ;;
    40→  esac
    41→done
    42→
    43→# Read HTML content from stdin
    44→HTML_CONTENT=$(cat)
    45→
    46→if [[ -z "$HTML_CONTENT" ]]; then
    47→  error "No HTML content provided on stdin"
    48→  exit 1
    49→fi
    50→
    51→# Create output directory
    52→mkdir -p "$OUTPUT_DIR"
    53→
    54→# Generate filename if not provided
    55→if [[ -z "$FILENAME" ]]; then
    56→  FILENAME="trail-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).html"
    57→fi
    58→
    59→OUTPUT_PATH="${OUTPUT_DIR}/${FILENAME}"
    60→
    61→# Write the HTML file
    62→printf '%s' "$HTML_CONTENT" > "$OUTPUT_PATH"
    63→info "Wrote visualization to ${OUTPUT_PATH}"
    64→
    65→# Open in browser
    66→if [[ "$OPEN_BROWSER" == true ]]; then
    67→  if command -v open &>/dev/null; then
    68→    open "$OUTPUT_PATH"
    69→    info "Opened in browser"
    70→  elif command -v xdg-open &>/dev/null; then
    71→    xdg-open "$OUTPUT_PATH"
    72→    info "Opened in browser"
    73→  else
    74→    warn "Could not detect browser opener. File is at: ${OUTPUT_PATH}"
    75→  fi
    76→fi
    77→
    78→# Print the path for the caller
    79→echo "$OUTPUT_PATH"
    80→

1→#!/bin/bash
     2→# scripts/fly-pause.sh - Estimate TTS duration and pause for fly-through auto-advance
     3→# Used between fly-through steps to wait for narration to finish + buffer
     4→set -euo pipefail
     5→
     6→SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
     7→PLUGIN_ROOT="$(dirname "$SCRIPT_DIR")"
     8→
     9→source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/lib/utils.sh"
    10→source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/lib/config.sh"
    11→
    12→# Defaults
    13→WPM="${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_RATE:-200}"
    14→BUFFER=2
    15→TEXT=""
    16→
    17→# Parse arguments
    18→while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    19→  case $1 in
    20→    --wpm) WPM="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    21→    --buffer) BUFFER="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    22→    --help)
    23→      cat <<'EOF'
    24→Usage: fly-pause.sh [OPTIONS] [TEXT]
    25→
    26→Estimates TTS duration from word count and pauses for that duration plus a buffer.
    27→Used by fly-through mode to time auto-advance between code snippets.
    28→
    29→OPTIONS:
    30→  --wpm RATE      Words per minute (default: 200, from TTS config)
    31→  --buffer SECS   Additional seconds to wait after estimated duration (default: 2)
    32→
    33→ENVIRONMENT:
    34→  DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_RATE   WPM rate (same as tts.sh)
    35→EOF
    36→      exit 0
    37→      ;;
    38→    *) TEXT="$*"; break ;;
    39→  esac
    40→done
    41→
    42→# Read from stdin if no text argument
    43→if [[ -z "$TEXT" ]]; then
    44→  TEXT=$(cat)
    45→fi
    46→
    47→if [[ -z "$TEXT" ]]; then
    48→  error "No text provided"
    49→  exit 1
    50→fi
    51→
    52→# Load config for WPM if not overridden
    53→CONFIG_FILE="${PLUGIN_ROOT}/config/tts.json"
    54→if [[ -f "$CONFIG_FILE" ]]; then
    55→  CONFIG_BUFFER=$(jq -r '.fly_through.auto_pause_buffer // 2' "$CONFIG_FILE")
    56→  if [[ "$BUFFER" == "2" ]]; then
    57→    BUFFER="$CONFIG_BUFFER"
    58→  fi
    59→  if [[ -z "${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_RATE:-}" ]]; then
    60→    WPM=$(jq -r '.say.rate // 200' "$CONFIG_FILE")
    61→  fi
    62→fi
    63→
    64→# Count words
    65→WORD_COUNT=$(echo "$TEXT" | wc -w | tr -d ' ')
    66→
    67→# Calculate duration: (words / WPM) * 60 + buffer
    68→if [[ "$WORD_COUNT" -eq 0 ]]; then
    69→  DURATION="$BUFFER"
    70→else
    71→  # Use awk for floating point math
    72→  DURATION=$(awk "BEGIN {printf \"%.1f\", ($WORD_COUNT / $WPM) * 60 + $BUFFER}")
    73→fi
    74→
    75→debug "Word count: $WORD_COUNT, WPM: $WPM, Buffer: ${BUFFER}s, Total pause: ${DURATION}s"
    76→
    77→# Sleep for the calculated duration
    78→sleep "$DURATION"
    79→

1→---
     2→name: Narration
     3→description: This skill should be used when generating spoken explanations of code, diffs, or architecture. Provides guidelines for conversational, TTS-friendly narration that sounds like a staff engineer pairing with a new team member.
     4→version: 1.0.0
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Narration Skill
     8→
     9→Generate spoken narration for code walkthroughs and diff reviews. All narration will be piped to TTS, so it must sound natural when read aloud.
    10→
    11→## Core Principles
    12→
    13→1. **Conversational** — Talk like a helpful colleague, not a textbook. Use "Let's look at...", "Notice how...", "The reason for this is..."
    14→2. **Educational** — Explain WHY changes were made or WHY code is structured a way, not just WHAT the code does
    15→3. **Progressive** — Start high-level, let the user drill into detail on demand
    16→4. **Engaging** — Vary pacing, use transitions ("Now the interesting part is..."), highlight non-obvious decisions
    17→
    18→## TTS-Friendly Rules
    19→
    20→- Spell out abbreviations on first use: "JWT, or JSON Web Token"
    21→- Say "slash" for path separators: "server slash routes slash users dot ts"
    22→- Avoid reading code syntax literally. Never say "const x equals await..."
    23→- Use natural descriptions: "a function that validates user input" not "function validateUserInput parenthesis data colon string"
    24→- Keep sentences under 25 words for natural speech rhythm
    25→- Use punctuation to create pauses — commas, periods, em dashes
    26→- Avoid parenthetical asides (they break speech flow)
    27→
    28→## Narration Templates
    29→
    30→### Section Overview (Diff Review)
    31→```
    32→Let's look at [section title]. This is [critical/important/supporting].
    33→The changes here [describe purpose in plain language].
    34→[If breaking:] This is a breaking change because [reason].
    35→There are [N] files involved: [list files with "slash" separators].
    36→[If cross-cutting:] This impacts [areas of the codebase].
    37→```
    38→
    39→### Section Overview (Walkthrough)
    40→```
    41→Now we're looking at [area of codebase]. This is where [purpose].
    42→The key thing to understand is [core concept].
    43→You'll see [pattern] used here because [reason].
    44→The main files are [list naturally].
    45→```
    46→
    47→### Detailed Explanation
    48→```
    49→Looking at [filename]:
    50→[For each significant change or section:]
    51→- [Describe intent] by [describe approach]
    52→- [Highlight key decisions or trade-offs]
    53→- [Note edge cases or error handling]
    54→```
    55→
    56→### Transitions Between Sections
    57→```
    58→"Now let's move on to [next section]. This connects to what we just saw because..."
    59→"That covers the [previous topic]. Next up is [next topic], which is where..."
    60→"Good, so we understand [summary]. The next piece of the puzzle is..."
    61→```
    62→
    63→## What NOT to Do
    64→
    65→- Don't read code line by line: "Line 1 import express from express, line 2..."
    66→- Don't use jargon without context: "It implements memoization via closure capture"
    67→- Don't be vague: "Some changes were made to improve things"
    68→- Don't narrate boilerplate: skip import statements, config lines, formatting changes
    69→- Don't repeat yourself between overview and detail modes
    70→
    71→## Verbosity Levels
    72→
    73→### Brief
    74→2-3 sentences per section. Just the what and why. Skip how.
    75→
    76→### Normal (default)
    77→5-8 sentences per section. What, why, and key how. Mention patterns and decisions.
    78→
    79→### Detailed
    80→Full walkthrough. What, why, how, trade-offs, alternatives considered, edge cases. 10+ sentences.
    81→
    82→## Fly-Through Narration
    83→
    84→### Fly-Through Opening
    85→```
    86→Let's fly through the key code in this section. I'll show you N snippets, walking through the execution flow. Watch the code as I narrate.
    87→```
    88→
    89→### Fly-Through Step
    90→```
    91→[2-4 sentences per snippet. Reference visible code directly: "See that try-catch on line 23?"]
    92→[End with transition to next: "This calls into the service layer, which we'll see next."]
    93→```
    94→
    95→### Fly-Through Closing
    96→```
    97→That covers the key code paths for this section. We looked at N files, from [starting point] through to [ending point]. The main takeaway is [key insight].
    98→```
    99→
   100→## Quiz Narration
   101→
   102→### Quiz Introduction
   103→```
   104→Let's check your understanding of what we've covered. I'll ask N quick questions about the last few sections. Just answer in your own words — no need to be exact.
   105→```
   106→
   107→### Quiz Question
   108→```
   109→[Read the question slowly and clearly]
   110→[Pause to let the user think]
   111→```
   112→
   113→### Correct Answer Feedback
   114→```
   115→That's right. [1-2 sentences elaborating, referencing the code]. Look at [file:line] to see exactly where this happens.
   116→```
   117→
   118→### Partial Answer Feedback
   119→```
   120→You're on the right track. [Acknowledge what's correct]. The piece you're missing is [key concept]. Check [file:line] for the full picture.
   121→```
   122→
   123→### Incorrect Answer Feedback
   124→```
   125→Not quite. [Gentle explanation of the correct answer]. If you look back at [file:line], you can see [specific evidence]. The key thing is [main takeaway].
   126→```
   127→
   128→### Quiz Summary
   129→```
   130→Nice work — you got N out of M. [If high score: "You're tracking well."] [If low: "Let's keep going — these concepts will click as we see more code."] Your running total is X out of Y.
   131→```
   132→
   133→## References
   134→- `references/voice-personas.md` — Voice assignments and persona styles for team mode
   135→- `references/ranking.md` — Importance ranking criteria for ordering sections
   136→- `references/fly-through.md` — Fly-through snippet plan format and display template
   137→- `references/quiz.md` — Quiz question types, evaluation guidelines, and narration style
   138→

1→# Shared Instructions for diff-pair-review Commands
     2→
     3→These instructions are used by both `/diff-review` and `/walkthrough` commands.
     4→
     5→## TTS Integration
     6→
     7→To narrate an explanation:
     8→1. Generate conversational text following the `narration` skill guidelines
     9→2. Execute TTS asynchronously:
    10→   ```
    11→   Bash: ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh "narration text here"
    12→   ```
    13→3. Display the code/content in the terminal at the same time — don't wait for audio to finish
    14→
    15→To use a specific voice (team mode):
    16→```
    17→Bash: ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh --voice "Daniel" "narration text"
    18→```
    19→
    20→To use OpenAI TTS:
    21→```
    22→Bash: ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh --engine openai --voice nova "narration text"
    23→```
    24→
    25→## Interactive Navigation
    26→
    27→After displaying each section, present these commands and wait for user input:
    28→
    29→| Command | Action |
    30→|---------|--------|
    31→| `next` | Move to the next section |
    32→| `prev` | Go back to the previous section |
    33→| `detail` | Show full content for current section (complete diff or file-by-file explanation) |
    34→| `related` | Find and show files that import/use code from the current section |
    35→| `question <text>` | Answer a question in context of the current section |
    36→| `jump <N>` | Jump to section number N |
    37→| `list` | Show overview table of all sections with viewed/current status |
    38→| `done` | End the session with a summary |
    39→| `fly` | Enter fly-through for current section (auto-advancing code snippets with TTS) |
    40→| `pause` | Pause fly-through auto-advance |
    41→| `resume` | Resume fly-through auto-advance |
    42→| `stop` | Exit fly-through, return to section navigation |
    43→| `skip` | Skip the current quiz question |
    44→| `hint` | Get a hint for the current quiz question |
    45→| `score` | Show quiz score summary |
    46→
    47→### How to detect user commands
    48→The user's next message will be one of the above commands (possibly with extra text). Parse it:
    49→- Starts with "next" → advance section
    50→- Starts with "prev" → go back
    51→- Starts with "detail" → show detailed view
    52→- Starts with "related" → grep for connections
    53→- Starts with "question" → answer the rest of the message as a question
    54→- Starts with "jump" → extract number, go to that section
    55→- Starts with "list" → show overview
    56→- Starts with "done" → summarize and end
    57→- Starts with "fly" → enter fly-through mode for current section
    58→- Starts with "pause" → pause fly-through auto-advance
    59→- Starts with "resume" → resume fly-through auto-advance
    60→- Starts with "stop" → exit fly-through mode
    61→- Starts with "skip" → skip current quiz question
    62→- Starts with "hint" → show hint for current quiz question
    63→- Starts with "score" → show quiz score summary
    64→- Anything else → treat as a question about the current section
    65→
    66→## Terminal Display Formatting
    67→
    68→### Section Header
    69→```
    70→================================================================
    71→  [ICON] SECTION TITLE (Section N of M)
    72→================================================================
    73→```
    74→
    75→Icons by section type:
    76→- Overview: book icon or "OVERVIEW"
    77→- Architecture: folder icon or "ARCHITECTURE"
    78→- Entry Points: rocket or "ENTRY POINTS"
    79→- Core Logic: gear or "CORE LOGIC"
    80→- Data Layer: database or "DATA LAYER"
    81→- Integrations: plug or "INTEGRATIONS"
    82→- Testing: test tube or "TESTING"
    83→- Build/Deploy: rocket or "BUILD & DEPLOY"
    84→
    85→### Diff Display
    86→Show diffs with:
    87→- File path as header
    88→- Line numbers
    89→- + lines for additions, - lines for deletions
    90→- 3 lines of context around changes
    91→
    92→### Navigation Prompt
    93→Always end each section with:
    94→```
    95→Commands: next | fly | detail | related | question | list | done
    96→```
    97→
    98→## State Tracking
    99→
   100→Track the walkthrough state mentally across turns:
   101→- Current section index
   102→- Which sections have been viewed
   103→- Which sections have been detailed
   104→- The full list of sections with their content
   105→
   106→There is no external state file — Claude maintains state through conversation context.
   107→
   108→## Fly-Through Display
   109→
   110→When in fly-through mode, display each step using this template:
   111→
   112→### Step Display
   113→```
   114→────────────────────────────────────────────────
   115→  Step N of M — file/path.ts:15-38
   116→────────────────────────────────────────────────
   117→[code snippet displayed via Read tool]
   118→
   119→> 💬 [narration text]
   120→
   121→  ▶ Auto-advancing in Ns... (pause | stop)
   122→────────────────────────────────────────────────
   123→```
   124→
   125→The `file:line` format is clickable in VS Code terminals.
   126→
   127→### Fly-Through Flow
   128→1. Request snippet plan from narrator agent for current section
   129→2. For each snippet in the plan:
   130→   a. Display the step header with file:line range
   131→   b. Read and display the code using the Read tool
   132→   c. Display narration in a quote block
   133→   d. Trigger TTS with the narration text
   134→   e. Pause for estimated TTS duration + buffer (via `scripts/fly-pause.sh`)
   135→   f. Auto-advance to next snippet
   136→3. On `pause`: stop advancing, show "Paused at step N. Type resume or stop."
   137→4. On `resume`: continue from current step
   138→5. On `stop` or after last snippet: return to section navigation with "Fly-through complete. N snippets reviewed."
   139→
   140→### Fly-Through State
   141→Track within fly-through mode:
   142→- Current snippet index within the fly-through
   143→- Whether auto-advance is paused
   144→- The full snippet plan for the current section
   145→
   146→## Comprehension Quiz
   147→
   148→### Quiz Flow
   149→After every 2-3 sections (configurable), automatically trigger a quiz:
   150→
   151→1. Call quiz-generator agent with content from the recently covered sections
   152→2. Present each question one at a time:
   153→   ```
   154→   ════════════════════════════════════════════════
   155→     📝 COMPREHENSION CHECK (Question N of M)
   156→   ════════════════════════════════════════════════
   157→
   158→   [Question text]
   159→
   160→   (Type your answer, or: skip | hint)
   161→   ════════════════════════════════════════════════
   162→   ```
   163→3. Evaluate the user's answer (generously — partial credit for showing understanding)
   164→4. Provide feedback referencing specific code:
   165→   - Correct: "✓ That's right. [elaboration with file:line reference]"
   166→   - Partial: "~ On the right track. [what they got right + what they missed]"
   167→   - Incorrect: "✗ Not quite. [correct answer with file:line reference]"
   168→5. After all questions, show score summary
   169→6. Continue to next section
   170→
   171→### Quiz Score Display
   172→```
   173→────────────────────────────────────────────
   174→  Quiz Results: N/M correct
   175→  Running total: X/Y across all quizzes
   176→────────────────────────────────────────────
   177→```
   178→
   179→### Quiz State
   180→Track across the session:
   181→- Questions asked (total)
   182→- Questions answered correctly / partially / incorrectly
   183→- Which sections have been quizzed
   184→- Running score for the final summary
   185→

1→# Framework Trails — Trail Data Reference
     2→
     3→Trail definitions for each supported framework. Each trail maps the framework's request/render pipeline to ~8 stops on the Coderegon Trail.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Next.js — "The App Router Trail"
     8→
     9→### Trail Overview
    10→Follow a request from the browser through Next.js App Router's middleware, file-based routing, layout nesting, server/client component boundary, data fetching, and final response rendering.
    11→
    12→### Stops
    13→
    14→#### Stop 0: Independence, MO — The Entry Point
    15→- **concept**: Project initialization
    16→- **landmarkType**: town
    17→- **code**:
    18→  ```typescript
    19→  // app/layout.tsx:1-18
    20→  import type { Metadata } from 'next'
    21→  import './globals.css'
    22→
    23→  export const metadata: Metadata = {
    24→    title: 'My App',
    25→    description: 'Built with Next.js App Router',
    26→  }
    27→
    28→  export default function RootLayout({
    29→    children,
    30→  }: {
    31→    children: React.ReactNode
    32→  }) {
    33→    return (
    34→      <html lang="en">
    35→        <body>{children}</body>
    36→      </html>
    37→    )
    38→  }
    39→  ```
    40→- **narration**: "Every Next.js journey starts here — the root layout. This file wraps your entire application. Notice the metadata export: Next.js reads this at build time to set your HTML head. The children prop is where every page in your app gets rendered. Think of this as the wagon that carries everything."
    41→
    42→#### Stop 1: Middleware Pass
    43→- **concept**: Request interception
    44→- **landmarkType**: mountain
    45→- **code**:
    46→  ```typescript
    47→  // middleware.ts:1-20
    48→  import { NextResponse } from 'next/server'
    49→  import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'
    50→
    51→  export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
    52→    const token = request.cookies.get('session')
    53→
    54→    if (!token && request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith('/dashboard')) {
    55→      return NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/login', request.url))
    56→    }
    57→
    58→    const response = NextResponse.next()
    59→    response.headers.set('x-request-id', crypto.randomUUID())
    60→    return response
    61→  }
    62→
    63→  export const config = {
    64→    matcher: ['/dashboard/:path*', '/api/:path*'],
    65→  }
    66→  ```
    67→- **narration**: "Before any page renders, the request passes through middleware. This runs on the Edge Runtime — fast and lightweight. Here it checks for a session cookie. No token? Redirected to login. The matcher config at the bottom tells Next.js which routes this middleware applies to. It's your first line of defense."
    68→
    69→#### Stop 2: File Router Fork
    70→- **concept**: File-system routing
    71→- **landmarkType**: camp
    72→- **code**:
    73→  ```
    74→  // File system IS the router:
    75→  app/
    76→  ├── page.tsx              → /
    77→  ├── about/
    78→  │   └── page.tsx          → /about
    79→  ├── blog/
    80→  │   ├── page.tsx          → /blog

1→{
     2→  "name": "coderegon-trail",

1→#!/bin/bash
     2→# Configuration loader for diff-pair-review TTS
     3→# Priority: CLI flags > env vars > config file > defaults
     4→
     5→load_config() {
     6→  local config_file="${PLUGIN_ROOT}/config/tts.json"
     7→
     8→  if [[ ! -f "$config_file" ]]; then
     9→    debug "No config file found at $config_file, using defaults"
    10→    return
    11→  fi
    12→
    13→  debug "Loading config from $config_file"
    14→
    15→  if [[ -z "${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_ENGINE:-}" ]]; then
    16→    ENGINE=$(jq -r '.engine // "say"' "$config_file")
    17→  fi
    18→
    19→  if [[ -z "${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_VOICE:-}" ]]; then
    20→    if [[ "$ENGINE" == "say" ]]; then
    21→      VOICE=$(jq -r '.say.voice // "Samantha"' "$config_file")
    22→    else
    23→      VOICE=$(jq -r '.openai.voice // "alloy"' "$config_file")
    24→    fi
    25→  fi
    26→
    27→  if [[ -z "${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_RATE:-}" ]]; then
    28→    RATE=$(jq -r '.say.rate // 200' "$config_file")
    29→  fi
    30→
    31→  if [[ -z "${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_SPEED:-}" ]]; then
    32→    SPEED=$(jq -r '.openai.speed // 1.0' "$config_file")
    33→  fi
    34→
    35→  if [[ -z "${DIFF_REVIEW_TTS_MODEL:-}" ]]; then
    36→    MODEL=$(jq -r '.openai.model // "tts-1"' "$config_file")
    37→  fi
    38→}
    39→

1→---
     2→description: "Walk through code changes with TTS-narrated explanation"
     3→argument-hint: "[PR# | URL | branch | range | --team]"
     4→allowed-tools:
     5→  - Bash(gh:*)
     6→  - Bash(git:*)
     7→  - Bash(say:*)
     8→  - Bash(afplay:*)
     9→  - Bash(curl:*)
    10→  - Bash(${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/*:*)
    11→  - Read
    12→  - Grep
    13→  - Glob
    14→  - Task
    15→model: opus
    16→---
    17→
    18→# Diff Review — Narrated Code Change Walkthrough
    19→
    20→You are a staff engineer conducting a friendly, educational code review walkthrough. You explain changes to a teammate who is new to the codebase — focusing on WHY changes matter, not just what changed.
    21→
    22→**User input:** "$ARGUMENTS"
    23→
    24→Use the `narration` skill for all spoken explanations. Follow `lib/shared-instructions.md` for navigation and display formatting.
    25→
    26→## Step 1: Parse Arguments and Determine Diff Source
    27→
    28→Determine the diff source from the user's input:
    29→
    30→1. If input contains `github.com` and `/pull/` → extract PR number from URL. Use `gh pr diff <number>`.
    31→2. If input is a plain number → GitHub PR. Use `gh pr diff <number>`.
    32→3. If input contains `..` → git range. Use `git diff <range>`.
    33→4. If input matches `HEAD~N` → last N commits. Use `git diff HEAD~N..HEAD`.
    34→5. If input is a branch name (verify with `git rev-parse --verify`) → branch comparison. Use `git diff <branch>..HEAD`.
    35→6. If input is empty or only flags → uncommitted changes. Use `git diff HEAD`. If that's empty, try `git diff --cached`. If still empty, say there are no changes to review.
    36→
    37→Check for the `--team` flag. If present, follow the Team Mode section below.
    38→Check for the `--no-quiz` flag. If present, disable comprehension quizzes for this session.
    39→
    40→Also fetch context about the change:
    41→- For PRs: run `gh pr view <number>` to get title, description, author
    42→- For branches: run `git log --oneline <range>` to get commit messages
    43→
    44→## Step 2: Fetch and Analyze the Diff
    45→
    46→Run the appropriate git/gh command to get the full diff.
    47→
    48→Then analyze it to create logical sections:
    49→
    50→### Grouping Algorithm
    51→1. List all changed files from the diff
    52→2. **Group by feature first**: files that change together for a single purpose
    53→   - Same directory + related names (e.g., `user.ts`, `user.test.ts`, `user.types.ts`)
    54→   - Files that reference the same new symbols (grep for new function/class names)
    55→3. **Then group by layer**: if files don't feature-group, group by architectural layer
    56→   - All route files together, all service files together, etc.
    57→4. **Label each group** with a descriptive title: "New authentication module" not "Changes to auth.ts"
    58→
    59→### Importance Ranking
    60→Rank each section 1-7 following `skills/narration/references/ranking.md`:
    61→1. Core business logic (highest)
    62→2. Breaking API changes
    63→3. Data model/schema changes
    64→4. Non-breaking API additions
    65→5. Utility/helper changes
    66→6. Test changes
    67→7. Config/build/dependencies (lowest)
    68→
    69→Apply boost/reduce factors from the ranking reference.
    70→
    71→Sort sections by rank (most important first).
    72→
    73→## Step 3: Present Overview
    74→
    75→Display a summary of all sections:
    76→
    77→```
    78→================================================================
    79→  DIFF REVIEW: [PR title or branch description]
    80→================================================================
    81→
    82→[N] sections ranked by importance:
    83→
    84→1. *** [Section title] ([files], +X/-Y lines)
    85→2. **  [Section title] ([files], +X/-Y lines)
    86→3. *   [Section title] ([files], +X/-Y lines)
    87→...
    88→
    89→Starting with the most important change...
    90→```
    91→
    92→Stars indicate importance: *** = critical, ** = important, * = notable, (none) = supporting.
    93→
    94→## Step 4: Walk Through First Section
    95→
    96→For the highest-ranked section:
    97→
    98→1. **Display the section header** with file names and change stats
    99→2. **Show a diff excerpt** — the most important hunk(s), not the entire diff. Use 3 lines of context.
   100→3. **Generate narration** following the narration skill:
   101→   - What changed and why it matters
   102→   - How the changes work at a high level
   103→   - Any patterns, trade-offs, or notable decisions
   104→   - Keep it conversational: "Let's start with the most important change..."
   105→4. **Trigger TTS**: Run `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh "narration text"`
   106→5. **Show navigation prompt**: `Commands: next | fly | detail | related | question | list | done`
   107→
   108→## Step 5: Handle Navigation
   109→
   110→When the user responds, parse their command and act:
   111→
   112→### next
   113→- Move to the next section (increment index)
   114→- Generate narration with a transition: "Now let's move on to..."
   115→- Show the next section's diff excerpt
   116→- Trigger TTS
   117→- If on the last section, say so and suggest `done`
   118→
   119→### prev
   120→- Move back one section
   121→- Re-display with narration
   122→- If on the first section, say so
   123→
   124→### detail
   125→- Show the FULL diff for every file in the current section
   126→- Generate detailed line-by-line narration (following narration skill "Detailed Explanation" template)
   127→- Explain each significant change, skip boilerplate
   128→- Trigger TTS with detailed narration
   129→
   130→### related
   131→- Find files that import or reference code from the current section:
   132→  - Grep for function/class names exported by these files
   133→  - Grep for import statements referencing these files
   134→- Display the connections: "TasksService is imported by TasksController (routes/tasks.ts:5)"
   135→- Narrate how the current changes ripple through the codebase
   136→
   137→### question <text>
   138→- Read the user's question
   139→- Answer it in context of the current section's files and diff
   140→- If needed, read additional files for context
   141→- Narrate the answer via TTS
   142→
   143→### jump <N>
   144→- Validate N is a valid section number
   145→- Jump to that section
   146→- Display and narrate it
   147→
   148→### list
   149→- Show the overview table with status indicators:
   150→  ```
   151→  #  | Imp. | Title                    | Files | Status
   152→  ---|------|--------------------------|-------|--------
   153→  1  | ***  | Auth module              | 2     | Viewed
   154→  2  | **   | User model update        | 2     | >> Current
   155→  3  | *    | Profile tests            | 1     |
   156→  ```
   157→
   158→### fly
   159→- Enter fly-through mode for the current section:
   160→  1. Call the diff-narrator agent (via Task tool, subagent_type: general-purpose) requesting a fly-through snippet plan for the current section's files and diff
   161→  2. Parse the `SNIPPET_PLAN:` response into an ordered list of snippets
   162→  3. For each snippet, execute the fly-through step:
   163→     a. Display the step header: `Step N of M — file/path.ts:start-end`
   164→     b. Use the Read tool to display the code at the specified file and line range
   165→     c. Show the narration in a quote block below the code
   166→     d. Trigger TTS: `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh "narration text"`
   167→     e. Pause for TTS duration: `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/fly-pause.sh "narration text"`
   168→     f. Auto-advance to the next snippet
   169→  4. If user types `pause` — stop auto-advancing, show "Paused at step N. Type resume or stop."
   170→  5. If user types `resume` — continue from current step
   171→  6. If user types `stop` or the last snippet completes — return to section navigation
   172→  7. Show: "Fly-through complete. N snippets reviewed."
   173→- Follow the display template in `lib/shared-instructions.md` "Fly-Through Display" section
   174→
   175→### done
   176→- Display a summary:
   177→  - How many sections were reviewed
   178→  - Key takeaways (1-2 sentences about the overall change)
   179→  - Any concerns or suggestions raised during the walkthrough
   180→  - If quizzes were taken: show final score summary (total correct / total asked)
   181→- Narrate a brief closing: "That wraps up our review. The main thing to remember is..."
   182→
   183→## Team Mode
   184→
   185→When `--team` is passed:
   186→
   187→1. Launch a `domain-splitter` agent (via Task tool, subagent_type: general-purpose) to analyze the diff and split changed files into 2-4 domains
   188→2. For each domain, assign a voice from `skills/narration/references/voice-personas.md`
   189→3. The lead voice (Samantha/alloy) opens with the overview and provides transitions
   190→4. For each domain's sections, use that domain's assigned voice for TTS:
   191→   ```
   192→   ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh --voice "Daniel" "narration text"
   193→   ```
   194→5. The lead voice closes with a summary tying all domains together
   195→
   196→The navigation commands work the same — but each section is narrated in the voice of the domain specialist who owns it.
   197→
   198→## Comprehension Quiz
   199→
   200→After every 2-3 sections (configurable in `config/tts.json` key `quiz.sections_between_quizzes`), automatically trigger a comprehension check — unless `--no-quiz` was passed.
   201→
   202→### Quiz Trigger
   203→Track how many sections the user has viewed since the last quiz (or since the start). When the count reaches the configured threshold:
   204→
   205→1. **Generate questions**: Call the quiz-generator agent (via Task tool, subagent_type: general-purpose) with:
   206→   - The code snippets and diff excerpts shown in the recent sections
   207→   - The narration text generated for those sections
   208→   - The section topics and key concepts
   209→   - Request 3 questions (configurable in `config/tts.json` key `quiz.questions_per_break`)
   210→2. **Parse the `QUIZ:` response** into ordered questions with type, difficulty, reference, key_points, and hint
   211→3. **Present each question** one at a time following the quiz display template in `lib/shared-instructions.md`
   212→4. **Trigger TTS** for each question: `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh "question text"`
   213→5. **Wait for user response**:
   214→   - If user types `skip` → move to next question, mark as skipped
   215→   - If user types `hint` → show the hint text, re-prompt for answer
   216→   - Otherwise → evaluate the answer against key_points
   217→6. **Provide feedback** with TTS following the narration skill quiz templates:
   218→   - Correct: acknowledge and elaborate with file:line reference
   219→   - Partial: acknowledge what's right, explain what's missing
   220→   - Incorrect: gently redirect with the correct answer and file:line reference
   221→   - If the user's answer references code no longer visible, re-show the relevant snippet via Read
   222→7. **After all questions**: show score summary and running total
   223→8. **Continue** to the next section
   224→
   225→### Team Mode Quiz
   226→In team mode, each domain specialist asks questions about their domain using their voice. The lead can ask cross-domain questions.
   227→
   228→## Narration Quality Checklist
   229→
   230→Before generating any narration, verify:
   231→- [ ] Uses conversational language, not code-reading
   232→- [ ] Explains WHY, not just WHAT
   233→- [ ] Spells out abbreviations on first use
   234→- [ ] Uses "slash" for path separators in speech
   235→- [ ] Sentences under 25 words
   236→- [ ] Skips boilerplate (imports, formatting)
   237→- [ ] Includes transitions between sections
   238→

1→---
     2→description: "Walk through an unfamiliar codebase with TTS-narrated explanation"
     3→argument-hint: "[dir | topic | --quick | --team]"
     4→allowed-tools:
     5→  - Bash(git:*)
     6→  - Bash(say:*)
     7→  - Bash(afplay:*)
     8→  - Bash(curl:*)
     9→  - Bash(${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/*:*)
    10→  - Read
    11→  - Grep
    12→  - Glob
    13→  - Task
    14→model: opus
    15→---
    16→
    17→# Walkthrough — Narrated Codebase Tour
    18→
    19→You are a staff engineer onboarding a new team member by giving them a guided tour of the codebase. You explain the architecture, patterns, and key files — focusing on building mental models, not memorizing code.
    20→
    21→**User input:** "$ARGUMENTS"
    22→
    23→Use the `narration` skill for all spoken explanations. Follow `lib/shared-instructions.md` for navigation and display formatting.
    24→
    25→## Step 1: Parse Arguments
    26→
    27→Determine the walkthrough scope:
    28→
    29→1. **Empty** → full repo walkthrough (all 8 sections)
    30→2. **Directory path** (contains `/` or matches a real directory) → scoped walkthrough of that directory
    31→3. **`--quick`** flag → abbreviated overview (sections 0-2 only)
    32→4. **`--team`** flag → team mode with multiple voices (see Team Mode below)
    33→5. **`--no-quiz`** flag → disable comprehension quizzes for this session
    34→6. **Anything else** → treat as a topic. Grep for files related to the topic, scope walkthrough to those files.
    35→
    36→Flags can combine: `/walkthrough src/api --quick` or `/walkthrough --team --quick`
    37→
    38→## Step 2: Analyze the Codebase
    39→
    40→Launch analysis to understand the codebase. Use Task tool to spawn up to 3 Explore-type agents in parallel:
    41→
    42→### Agent 1: Architecture Explorer
    43→Prompt: "Analyze the project structure, tech stack, and high-level architecture. Read README, package.json/pyproject.toml/go.mod/Cargo.toml, and directory structure. Identify the project type, primary language, key dependencies, and architectural pattern. Return a structured summary with the 8-10 most important files."
    44→
    45→### Agent 2: Entry Point Tracer
    46→Prompt: "Find all entry points (main files, index files, API route definitions, CLI commands) and trace how requests or executions flow through the system. Map the middleware stack, routing, and key call chains. Return the 5-8 most important files for understanding execution flow."
    47→
    48→### Agent 3: Pattern Analyzer
    49→Prompt: "Identify design patterns, code conventions, and architectural abstractions used in this codebase. Look for patterns like MVC, repository, service layer, dependency injection, event-driven, etc. Also identify error handling approaches, testing strategies, and common utilities. Return the 5-8 most important files showing key patterns."
    50→
    51→If scoped to a directory or topic, add the scope constraint to each agent prompt.
    52→
    53→Wait for all agents, then synthesize their findings.
    54→
    55→## Step 3: Build Walkthrough Sections
    56→
    57→Organize findings into up to 8 sections in pedagogical order:
    58→
    59→### Section 0: Project Overview
    60→- What the project does (1-2 sentences)
    61→- Tech stack (language, framework, database, etc.)
    62→- Project type (web app, CLI, library, API, etc.)
    63→- Size and age (file count, line count if relevant)
    64→- Key files to know about
    65→
    66→### Section 1: Directory Architecture
    67→- ASCII tree of top-level directory layout
    68→- Purpose of each major directory (1 line each)
    69→- Architectural boundaries (frontend/backend, core/plugins, etc.)
    70→- Where to find different types of code
    71→
    72→### Section 2: Entry Points & Request Flow
    73→- Application entry points
    74→- How requests/executions flow through the system
    75→- Middleware or interceptor chain
    76→- Routing/dispatch mechanism
    77→- Trace one example request end-to-end
    78→
    79→### Section 3: Core Domain Logic
    80→- Main business logic location
    81→- Service layer / use cases
    82→- Key algorithms or domain models
    83→- Where the "real work" happens
    84→
    85→### Section 4: Data Layer
    86→- Database/storage approach
    87→- Models and schemas
    88→- Migration strategy
    89→- Data access patterns (ORM, raw SQL, repositories)
    90→
    91→### Section 5: External Integrations
    92→- Third-party APIs and services
    93→- Message queues / event systems
    94→- Integration patterns (clients, adapters, wrappers)
    95→
    96→### Section 6: Testing Approach
    97→- Test framework and organization
    98→- Test types (unit, integration, e2e)
    99→- Coverage expectations
   100→- Key test patterns and fixtures
   101→
   102→### Section 7: Build & Deploy
   103→- Build process and tools
   104→- Environment configuration
   105→- Deployment approach
   106→- CI/CD pipeline
   107→
   108→**Skip sections that don't apply** (e.g., skip "External Integrations" for a pure library with no external calls).
   109→
   110→**Quick mode**: Only present sections 0, 1, and 2.
   111→
   112→## Step 4: Present the Tour
   113→
   114→Start with the overview:
   115→
   116→```
   117→================================================================
   118→  CODEBASE WALKTHROUGH: [Project Name]
   119→================================================================
   120→
   121→Analyzed [N] key files across [M] directories.
   122→
   123→Sections:
   124→0. Project Overview
   125→1. Directory Architecture
   126→2. Entry Points & Request Flow
   127→3. Core Domain Logic
   128→4. Data Layer
   129→5. External Integrations
   130→6. Testing Approach
   131→7. Build & Deploy
   132→
   133→Starting with the big picture...
   134→```
   135→
   136→Then present Section 0:
   137→
   138→1. **Display** the project overview with key facts
   139→2. **Generate narration**: "Welcome to [project name]. This is a [type] built with [stack]. It [primary purpose]. The codebase is organized into [N] main areas..."
   140→3. **Trigger TTS**: `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh "narration text"`
   141→4. **Navigation prompt**: `Commands: next | fly | detail | question | list | done`
   142→
   143→## Step 5: Handle Navigation
   144→
   145→Same commands as diff-review, adapted for walkthrough context:
   146→
   147→### next / prev
   148→Move between sections. Generate transitions: "That covers the architecture. Now let's look at how requests actually flow through the system..."
   149→
   150→### detail
   151→For a walkthrough section, "detail" means:
   152→- Show the actual code of key files in the section
   153→- Explain the code structure and important functions
   154→- Annotate with line ranges and purposes
   155→- Generate detailed narration walking through the file
   156→
   157→Can also target a specific file: if user says "detail server/index.js", read and explain that specific file.
   158→
   159→### related
   160→Show how the current section connects to other parts:
   161→- What imports from this area
   162→- What this area depends on
   163→- Data flow in and out
   164→
   165→### question
   166→Answer questions about the current section with full file context.
   167→
   168→### list
   169→Show section overview with status:
   170→```
   171→#  | Section                   | Status
   172→---|---------------------------|--------
   173→0  | Project Overview          | Viewed
   174→1  | Directory Architecture    | >> Current
   175→2  | Entry Points              |
   176→3  | Core Domain Logic         |
   177→...
   178→```
   179→
   180→### fly
   181→- Enter fly-through mode for the current section:
   182→  1. Call the codebase-narrator agent (via Task tool, subagent_type: general-purpose) requesting a fly-through snippet plan for the current section's key files
   183→  2. Parse the `SNIPPET_PLAN:` response into an ordered list of snippets
   184→  3. For each snippet, execute the fly-through step:
   185→     a. Display the step header: `Step N of M — file/path.ts:start-end`
   186→     b. Use the Read tool to display the code at the specified file and line range
   187→     c. Show the narration in a quote block below the code
   188→     d. Trigger TTS: `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh "narration text"`
   189→     e. Pause for TTS duration: `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/fly-pause.sh "narration text"`
   190→     f. Auto-advance to the next snippet
   191→  4. If user types `pause` — stop auto-advancing, show "Paused at step N. Type resume or stop."
   192→  5. If user types `resume` — continue from current step
   193→  6. If user types `stop` or the last snippet completes — return to section navigation
   194→  7. Show: "Fly-through complete. N snippets reviewed."
   195→- Follow the display template in `lib/shared-instructions.md` "Fly-Through Display" section
   196→
   197→### done
   198→Summarize the walkthrough:
   199→- Key architectural decisions
   200→- Most important files to remember
   201→- Suggested areas for deeper exploration
   202→- If quizzes were taken: show final score summary (total correct / total asked)
   203→- Narrate a closing: "That's the tour. The key things to remember are..."
   204→
   205→## Team Mode
   206→
   207→When `--team` is passed:
   208→
   209→1. After analysis (Step 2), launch a `domain-splitter` agent to divide the codebase into 2-4 logical domains based on the findings
   210→2. Assign voices from `skills/narration/references/voice-personas.md`:
   211→   - Lead (Samantha/alloy): Sections 0, 1, transitions, and closing
   212→   - Domain specialists: Each narrates the sections relevant to their domain
   213→3. The lead opens: "Welcome to the codebase. I'll give you the big picture, then hand off to our specialists..."
   214→4. For domain-specific sections, use the specialist's voice:
   215→   ```
   216→   ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh --voice "Daniel" "The backend follows a layered architecture..."
   217→   ```
   218→5. Specialists can reference each other: "The data layer, which Tom will cover next, provides the models we use here."
   219→6. Lead closes with a synthesis tying all domains together
   220→
   221→## Scope Filtering
   222→
   223→### Directory scope (`/walkthrough src/api`)
   224→- Restrict all analysis to that directory
   225→- Provide 1-2 sentences of project context first
   226→- Skip irrelevant sections (no "Frontend" section when looking at backend code)
   227→- Sections adapt to show what's in that directory
   228→
   229→### Topic scope (`/walkthrough auth`)
   230→- Grep for files containing the topic keyword
   231→- Also check directory names, function names, file names
   232→- Build sections around how that topic flows through the codebase
   233→- Might collapse into 3-4 sections instead of 8
   234→
   235→### Quick mode (`/walkthrough --quick`)
   236→- Only sections 0, 1, 2
   237→- Briefer narration (2-3 sentences each)
   238→- Total time: a few minutes instead of a full tour
   239→
   240→## Comprehension Quiz
   241→
   242→After every 2-3 sections (configurable in `config/tts.json` key `quiz.sections_between_quizzes`), automatically trigger a comprehension check — unless `--no-quiz` was passed.
   243→
   244→### Quiz Trigger
   245→Track how many sections the user has viewed since the last quiz (or since the start). When the count reaches the configured threshold:
   246→
   247→1. **Generate questions**: Call the quiz-generator agent (via Task tool, subagent_type: general-purpose) with:
   248→   - The code snippets shown in the recent sections
   249→   - The narration text generated for those sections
   250→   - The section topics and key concepts
   251→   - Request 3 questions (configurable in `config/tts.json` key `quiz.questions_per_break`)
   252→2. **Parse the `QUIZ:` response** into ordered questions with type, difficulty, reference, key_points, and hint
   253→3. **Present each question** one at a time following the quiz display template in `lib/shared-instructions.md`
   254→4. **Trigger TTS** for each question: `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tts.sh "question text"`
   255→5. **Wait for user response**:
   256→   - If user types `skip` → move to next question, mark as skipped
   257→   - If user types `hint` → show the hint text, re-prompt for answer
   258→   - Otherwise → evaluate the answer against key_points
   259→6. **Provide feedback** with TTS following the narration skill quiz templates:
   260→   - Correct: acknowledge and elaborate with file:line reference
   261→   - Partial: acknowledge what's right, explain what's missing
   262→   - Incorrect: gently redirect with the correct answer and file:line reference
   263→   - If the user's answer references code no longer visible, re-show the relevant snippet via Read
   264→7. **After all questions**: show score summary and running total
   265→8. **Continue** to the next section
   266→
   267→### Team Mode Quiz
   268→In team mode, each domain specialist asks questions about their domain using their voice. The lead can ask cross-domain questions.
   269→

74→## Trail Data Format
    75→
    76→Trail data is injected as a JavaScript object. The @frontend-design agent receives this data and embeds it in the HTML.
    77→
    78→```javascript
    79→const TRAIL_DATA = {
    80→  framework: "nextjs",
    81→  trailName: "The App Router Trail",
    82→  stops: [
    83→    {
    84→      name: "Independence, MO",
    85→      subtitle: "The Starting Point",
    86→      concept: "Project initialization and entry point",
    87→      code: {
    88→        file: "app/layout.tsx",
    89→        startLine: 1,
    90→        endLine: 20,
    91→        content: "// actual code here...",
    92→        language: "typescript"
    93→      },
    94→      narration: "Every Next.js app starts with a root layout...",
    95→      landmarkType: "town"  // town | mountain | river | forest | desert | camp
    96→    }
    97→  ],
    98→  events: [
    99→    {
   100→      type: "weather",        // weather | river | encounter | misfortune | fortune
   101→      trigger: "after_stop",  // after_stop | random | milestone
   102→      triggerStop: 2,
   103→      title: "Storm Warning!",
   104→      text: "A storm is coming — your middleware is throwing unhandled errors!",
   105→      choices: [
   106→        { text: "Add try-catch to each handler", correct: false, explanation: "..." },
   107→        { text: "Use a global error middleware", correct: true, explanation: "..." },
   108→        { text: "Ignore it and hope for clear skies", correct: false, explanation: "..." }
   109→      ],
   110→      concept: "Error handling patterns",
   111→      difficulty: "easy"
   112→    }
   113→  ],
   114→  partyMembers: [
   115→    { name: "Server Components", icon: "server", maxHealth: 3 },
   116→    { name: "File Router", icon: "router", maxHealth: 3 },
   117→    { name: "Layouts", icon: "layout", maxHealth: 3 },
   118→    { name: "Data Fetching", icon: "data", maxHealth: 3 }
   119→  ],
   120→  deathMessages: [
   121→    "Here lies your app. It died of unhandled promise rejections.",
   122→    "Lost in the middleware — never found the route handler."
   123→  ]
   124→};
   125→```
   126→

1→---
     2→description: Generates comprehension questions from code review and walkthrough content to test understanding
     3→tools: Read, Grep, Glob
     4→model: sonnet
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Quiz Generator Agent
     8→
     9→You generate comprehension questions that test a learner's understanding of code they've just reviewed or walked through. You focus on the "why" and "how" — never trivia about function names or syntax.
    10→
    11→## Your Task
    12→
    13→Given recent section content (code snippets, narration, topics covered), generate a set of quiz questions.
    14→
    15→## Input
    16→You receive:
    17→- Code snippets that were shown (with file paths and line ranges)
    18→- Narration text that explained those snippets
    19→- Section topics and key concepts covered
    20→- Number of questions to generate (default: 3)
    21→
    22→## Question Types
    23→Mix these types (never two consecutive of the same type):
    24→- **Why**: "Why does this use X instead of Y?" — tests understanding of design decisions
    25→- **What-if**: "What would happen if X were changed?" — tests grasp of consequences
    26→- **Trace**: "If a user does X, what happens step by step?" — tests flow understanding
    27→- **Connect**: "Which component depends on this?" — tests architecture knowledge
    28→- **Spot-the-issue**: "Is there a potential problem with this approach?" — tests critical thinking
    29→
    30→## Output Format
    31→Output a QUIZ: block with ordered questions:
    32→```
    33→QUIZ:
    34→Q1 [type: why] [difficulty: easy] [ref: path/file.ts:42-58]
    35→  "Question text here?"
    36→  key_points: ["point1", "point2"]
    37→  hint: "Hint text here."
    38→
    39→Q2 [type: what-if] [difficulty: medium] [ref: path/file.ts:15-30]
    40→  ...
    41→```
    42→
    43→## Question Quality Guidelines
    44→- Test understanding, NEVER memorization
    45→- Reference specific code by file:line that was shown during the review
    46→- Each question should have 2-3 key points that constitute a good answer
    47→- Include a hint for each question (used if user asks for one)
    48→- Difficulty progression: start easier, end harder
    49→- Questions should be answerable from the code and narration shown — don't require outside knowledge
    50→- Prefer questions with nuanced answers over yes/no questions
    51→
    52→## Answer Key Points
    53→For each question, provide key_points — these are the concepts the evaluator should look for in the user's answer. They should be:
    54→- Conceptual, not verbatim — the user doesn't need to use exact words
    55→- 2-3 points per question
    56→- Ordered by importance (most important first)
    57→
    58→## Team Mode
    59→When generating questions for team mode reviews:
    60→- Each domain specialist's questions should focus on their domain
    61→- The lead can ask cross-cutting questions that connect multiple domains
    62→- Tag each question with the domain it belongs to
    63→

toolUseResult.prompt:
You are the PR Trail Data Extractor agent. Analyze the git range `main..HEAD` in the current repo at /Users/flowclub/code/oreorg-trail and produce structured TRAIL_DATA JSON for the Coderegon Trail game.

This PR branch `pr-trail-game` builds the entire Coderegon Trail plugin — a retro pixel art Oregon Trail-style game that teaches web framework request pipelines. The branch has 20 commits and 43 files changed with ~31k insertions.

## Your Process

1. Get the commit log and diff stat:
```bash
git log --oneline main..HEAD
git diff main..HEAD --stat
```

2. Read key files to understand the changes. Focus on the most important/interesting files — NOT the example HTML games. Group changes into logical change groups.

3. For each stop, read the actual file content (NOT diff) to get a representative code snippet of 15-25 lines.

4. Build 6-8 stops ordered by importance, with the first being "Trailhead" (overview) and last being destination-themed.

5. Create quiz events that test understanding of THIS codebase — what the plugin does, how the game works, how the skill/agent/command architecture fits together.

6. Create 4 party members representing key domains of this PR.

7. Create death messages specific to this PR.

8. Derive a trail name from the branch's theme.

## Key Architecture Context

The repo is a Claude Code plugin (markdown specs + shell scripts):
- `commands/` — user-facing CLI commands (markdown with YAML frontmatter)
- `agents/` — Claude-powered analysis agents
- `skills/` — visualization and narration skill references
- `scripts/` — shell infrastructure (TTS, file opening)
- `examples/` — generated HTML game files

## Output

Return ONLY a JSON block in this exact format — start with `TRAIL_DATA:` on its own line, then the JSON:

```
TRAIL_DATA:
{
  "trailTheme": "pr",
  "framework": "pr",
  "trailName": "The ... Trail",
  "winDestination": "The Merge Frontier",
  "prSource": "main..HEAD",
  "prTitle": "Add Coderegon Trail game plugin",
  "stops": [...],
  "events": [...],
  "partyMembers": [...],
  "deathMessages": [...]
}
```

Each stop needs: name, subtitle, concept, landmarkType, code (with file, startLine, endLine, content, language), narration.
Each event needs: type, trigger ("after_stop"), triggerStop (index), title, text, choices (3 with text/correct/explanation), concept, difficulty.
Each party member needs: name, icon, maxHealth (3).

Make the quiz questions genuinely educational about how THIS plugin works — the markdown-as-spec architecture, TTS system, game state machine, trail data format, etc. Mix difficulties: 2 easy, 3 medium, 2 hard.

Narration should be conversational and TTS-friendly: sentences under 25 words, say "slash" for paths.

1→# Coderegon Trail — Pixel Art Style Guide
     2→
     3→Visual specifications for the Coderegon Trail game. All rendering is done programmatically on HTML5 Canvas (no image assets).
     4→
     5→## Color Palette
     6→
     7→### Primary Palette (8-bit inspired)
     8→```
     9→Sky:        #87CEEB (day), #1a1a2e (night), #FF6B35 (sunset)
    10→Ground:     #8B7355 (trail), #4a7c59 (grass), #c2b280 (desert)
    11→Mountains:  #6B7B8D (far), #4a5568 (near), #8B95A2 (snow caps)
    12→Trees:      #2d5016 (dark pine), #3d6b22 (light pine), #8B4513 (trunk)
    13→Water:      #4A90D9 (river), #357ABD (deep), #87CEEB (shallow)
    14→Wagon:      #8B4513 (wood), #D2B48C (canvas), #333333 (wheels)
    15→UI:         #1a1a2e (panel bg), #e2e8f0 (text), #48bb78 (health)
    16→```
    17→
    18→### Status Colors
    19→```
    20→Health:     #48bb78 (high), #ecc94b (medium), #f56565 (low)
    21→Morale:     #ffd700 (stars)
    22→Text:       #e2e8f0 (normal), #48bb78 (correct), #f56565 (wrong), #ecc94b (partial)
    23→Code:       #00ff41 (green phosphor) on #0a0a0a (near-black)
    24→```
    25→
    26→### Syntax Highlighting (Code Panel)
    27→```
    28→Keywords:   #ff79c6 (pink — function, const, export, async, await, return, if, else)
    29→Strings:    #f1fa8c (yellow — quoted text)
    30→Comments:   #6272a4 (muted blue — // and /* */)
    31→Default:    #f8f8f2 (off-white — everything else)
    32→Numbers:    #bd93f9 (purple)
    33→```
    34→
    35→## Layout Specifications
    36→
    37→### Screen Regions (top to bottom)
    38→
    39→```
    40→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    41→│  LANDSCAPE REGION (canvas)     40% of height │  ← scrolling terrain + wagon
    42→├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    43→│  CODE/NARRATION PANEL (DOM)    45% of height │  ← code snippet or event text
    44→├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    45→│  STATUS BAR (DOM)              15% of height │  ← health, supplies, party
    46→└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    47→```
    48→
    49→### Landscape Region (Canvas)
    50→
    51→- **Sky layer** (top 40%): Gradient sky with optional clouds, sun/moon
    52→- **Mountain layer** (20-40%): Parallax mountains, scroll at 0.3x speed
    53→- **Tree layer** (40-60%): Parallax trees/features, scroll at 0.6x speed
    54→- **Ground layer** (bottom 30%): Trail path, grass, rocks, scroll at 1x speed
    55→- **Wagon** (centered on trail): Animated wheels, framework logo as flag
    56→
    57→#### Parallax Scrolling
    58→- 3 layers scroll at different speeds for depth effect
    59→- Mountains: 0.3x wagon speed
    60→- Trees/features: 0.6x wagon speed
    61→- Ground: 1.0x wagon speed
    62→- Creates convincing depth with minimal rendering cost
    63→
    64→#### Landmark Types
    65→Each stop has a landmark rendered on the landscape:
    66→
    67→| Type | Visual |
    68→|------|--------|
    69→| town | Cluster of pixel buildings, signpost with stop name |
    70→| mountain | Prominent peak with snow cap |
    71→| river | Animated blue water band across the trail |
    72→| forest | Dense tree cluster |
    73→| desert | Sandy ground, cacti, minimal trees |
    74→| camp | Campfire, tent, small clearing |
    75→
    76→### Code/Narration Panel (DOM)
    77→
    78→#### Code Display Mode
    79→```css
    80→.code-panel {
    81→  background: #0a0a0a;
    82→  border: 2px solid #333;
    83→  border-radius: 4px;
    84→  padding: 16px;
    85→  font-family: 'SF Mono', 'Fira Code', 'Consolas', monospace;
    86→  font-size: 13px;
    87→  line-height: 1.5;
    88→  overflow-x: auto;
    89→}
    90→
    91→.code-header {
    92→  color: #6272a4;
    93→  font-size: 11px;
    94→  margin-bottom: 8px;
    95→  /* Shows: // file/path.ts:15-38 */
    96→}
    97→```
    98→
    99→#### Narration Display Mode
   100→```css
   101→.narration-panel {
   102→  background: #1a1a2e;
   103→  border: 2px solid #333;
   104→  padding: 20px;
   105→  font-family: 'Press Start 2P', 'Courier New', monospace;
   106→  font-size: 14px;
   107→  line-height: 1.8;
   108→  color: #e2e8f0;
   109→}
   110→```
   111→
   112→#### Event/Choice Display Mode
   113→```css
   114→.event-panel {
   115→  background: #1a1a2e;
   116→  border: 2px solid #ffd700;  /* Gold border for events */
   117→  padding: 20px;
   118→}
   119→
   120→.choice-button {
   121→  background: #2d3748;
   122→  border: 2px solid #4a5568;
   123→  color: #e2e8f0;
   124→  padding: 12px 20px;
   125→  margin: 8px 0;
   126→  cursor: pointer;
   127→  font-family: inherit;
   128→  font-size: 14px;
   129→  width: 100%;
   130→  text-align: left;
   131→  transition: border-color 0.2s;
   132→}
   133→
   134→.choice-button:hover {
   135→  border-color: #63b3ed;
   136→}
   137→
   138→.choice-button.correct {
   139→  border-color: #48bb78;
   140→  background: #1a3a2a;
   141→}
   142→
   143→.choice-button.incorrect {
   144→  border-color: #f56565;
   145→  background: #3a1a1a;
   146→}
   147→```
   148→
   149→### Status Bar (DOM)
   150→
   151→```
   152→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   153→│ Health: ████████░░ 80 │ Supplies: 5 │ ★★★   │
   154→│ Party: Concept1 ✓  Concept2 ✓  Concept3 ✓   │
   155→│ Stop 4 of 8 — [Current Stop Name]           │
   156→└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   157→```
   158→
   159→```css
   160→.status-bar {
   161→  background: #0d1117;
   162→  border-top: 2px solid #333;
   163→  padding: 12px 20px;
   164→  display: grid;
   165→  grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr;
   166→  gap: 8px;
   167→  font-family: 'SF Mono', monospace;
   168→  font-size: 12px;
   169→  color: #e2e8f0;
   170→}
   171→
   172→.health-bar {
   173→  height: 12px;
   174→  background: #2d3748;
   175→  border-radius: 2px;
   176→  overflow: hidden;
   177→}
   178→
   179→.health-fill {
   180→  height: 100%;
   181→  transition: width 0.5s ease;
   182→  /* Color changes based on health level */
   183→}
   184→```
   185→
   186→## Pixel Art Rendering (Canvas)
   187→
   188→### Wagon Sprite (drawn programmatically)
   189→
   190→```javascript
   191→function drawWagon(ctx, x, y, frameworkColor) {
   192→  // Wheels (2 circles with spokes)
   193→  // Body (rectangle with wooden plank texture)
   194→  // Canvas cover (arc with cloth texture)
   195→  // Framework flag (small triangle at top with framework color)
   196→  // Oxen (2 simple pixel rectangles in front)
   197→}
   198→```
   199→
   200→- Wagon width: ~60px at game scale
   201→- Wheel animation: rotate based on travel state
   202→- Framework flag color: framework-specific accent color
   203→
   204→### Framework Accent Colors
   205→```
   206→Next.js:   #000000 (black) with white text
   207→Rails:     #CC0000 (red)
   208→Django:    #092E20 (dark green)
   209→Express:   #353535 (dark grey)
   210→React:     #61DAFB (cyan)
   211→Laravel:   #FF2D20 (red-orange)
   212→```
   213→
   214→### Tree Drawing
   215→
   216→```javascript
   217→function drawPineTree(ctx, x, y, size) {
   218→  // Triangle layers (3 overlapping, decreasing size)
   219→  // Brown trunk rectangle
   220→  // Slight size/shade variation for organic feel
   221→}
   222→```
   223→
   224→### Mountain Drawing
   225→
   226→```javascript
   227→function drawMountain(ctx, x, y, width, height, color) {
   228→  // Triangle with slight irregularity
   229→  // Snow cap (white triangle at peak, smaller)
   230→  // Layered for parallax (far = lighter, near = darker)
   231→}
   232→```
   233→
   234→### River Drawing
   235→
   236→```javascript
   237→function drawRiver(ctx, y, width, frame) {
   238→  // Animated blue band across the screen
   239→  // Wavy top and bottom edges (sin wave, animated by frame)
   240→  // Lighter blue highlights for flow effect
   241→}
   242→```
   243→
   244→## Typography
   245→
   246→### Pixel Font (for titles, event headers)
   247→- Use CSS with a chunky pixel aesthetic
   248→- Fallback: `'Courier New', monospace` with `font-weight: bold` and `letter-spacing: 2px`
   249→- Can use `@font-face` with a base64-encoded small pixel font if size permits
   250→
   251→### Body Text (narration, descriptions)
   252→- `font-family: 'SF Mono', 'Fira Code', 'Consolas', 'Courier New', monospace`
   253→- `font-size: 14px`
   254→- `line-height: 1.8` for readability
   255→- `letter-spacing: 0.5px`
   256→
   257→### Code Text
   258→- Same monospace stack
   259→- `font-size: 13px`
   260→- `line-height: 1.5`
   261→
   262→## CRT Effect (Optional Toggle)
   263→
   264→```css
   265→.crt-overlay {
   266→  position: fixed;
   267→  top: 0;
   268→  left: 0;
   269→  width: 100%;
   270→  height: 100%;
   271→  pointer-events: none;
   272→  z-index: 9999;
   273→  background: repeating-linear-gradient(
   274→    0deg,
   275→    rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15) 0px,
   276→    rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15) 1px,
   277→    transparent 1px,
   278→    transparent 3px
   279→  );
   280→}
   281→```
   282→
   283→Toggle via a small "CRT" button in the corner. Off by default.
   284→
   285→## Animations
   286→
   287→### Wagon Travel
   288→- Wagon centered horizontally
   289→- Landscape scrolls left behind the wagon
   290→- Wheel rotation synced to scroll speed
   291→- Gentle wagon bounce (1-2px vertical oscillation)
   292→
   293→### Arrival at Stop
   294→- Wagon decelerates (easing function)
   295→- Landmark appears and grows into view
   296→- Stop name banner slides down from top
   297→- Code panel slides up from bottom
   298→
   299→### Event Appearance
   300→- Screen flash (brief gold overlay)
   301→- Event panel slides in with border glow
   302→- Choices appear one by one with slight stagger (200ms each)
   303→
   304→### Answer Feedback
   305→- Correct: Green flash, choice pulses, health bar animates up, "+10" floats up
   306→- Wrong: Red flash, screen shake (2px, 200ms), health bar animates down, "-15" floats up
   307→- Party damage: Portrait flashes red, health pip disappears
   308→
   309→### Death Sequence
   310→- Screen slowly fades to dark
   311→- Tombstone rises from bottom center
   312→- Death message typewriter-animates onto tombstone
   313→- "Press any key" fades in after message completes
   314→
   315→### Win Sequence
   316→- Fireworks: random position colored pixel explosions (8-10 bursts)
   317→- Score card slides in from right
   318→- Stats count up from 0 to final values (satisfying animation)
   319→- Study recommendation typewriter-animates last
   320→
   321→## Sound (Web Audio API — Optional)
   322→
   323→All sounds are generated via Web Audio API oscillators. No audio files needed.
   324→
   325→| Event | Sound |
   326→|-------|-------|
   327→| Travel start | Rising 3-note arpeggio (C-E-G) |
   328→| Stop arrival | Descending chime (G-E-C) |
   329→| Correct answer | Happy jingle (C-E-G-C♯) |
   330→| Wrong answer | Descending buzz (low frequency) |
   331→| Party member death | Sad descending tone |
   332→| Win | Triumphant 8-bar melody |
   333→| Death | Low somber 4-note phrase |
   334→| River crossing | Water-like oscillation |
   335→| Event appear | Alert tone (2 quick beeps) |
   336→
   337→Toggle via a speaker icon in the corner. **Off by default** — opt-in.
   338→
   339→## Accessibility
   340→
   341→- All choice buttons are keyboard-navigable (Tab + Enter)
   342→- Arrow keys navigate choices
   343→- Code panel has proper contrast ratios (WCAG AA on dark backgrounds)
   344→- Status bar text has sufficient contrast
   345→- Screen reader: choices have aria-labels
   346→- No auto-playing sound
   347→
